claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about the child or children you're claiming for:**

1. **How old is your child (or children)?** This is important because:
   - Children under 16 typically qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
   - Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
   - Children aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please tell me:
- How many children are you claiming for?
- What are their names and ages?

This will help me guide you through the rest of the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're looking to claim Child Benefit for your son Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with you.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), his eligibility will depend on whether he's in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to know about Alex's education or training status:

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In approved education or training?** (This includes full-time non-advanced education, or approved vocational training)
2. **Recently left education or training?** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Which of these applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's currently in education. He's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which is a form of education. Now I need to check that it meets the specific requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are specifically listed as an approved (non-advanced) qualification for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education:

**Please confirm the following:**

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)
2. **Is his education funded or arranged by an employer, or does he have an agreed job with an employer to start after finishing?** (If yes, this would exclude him from eligibility)
3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (If yes, he would need to be financially dependent on you to qualify)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's studying full-time at the college. No, it's not funded by any employer - it's just regular sixth form. And no, he's not married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Alex's A-level studies at sixth form college meet all the education requirements:
- ✓ A-levels are approved non-advanced education
- ✓ He's studying full-time (12+ hours per week)
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not married

Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's work situation:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Note: If a young person aged 16-19 is working 24 hours or more per week, they wouldn't qualify for Child Benefit even if they're in education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work at all. He's just focused on his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is focused on his studies and not working, so that requirement is met.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?** Specifically:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax Credits
- Income Support

If he's receiving any of these benefits in his own name (not as part of your household claim), he wouldn't qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own right.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next check:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

This is important because:
- All apprenticeships in England disqualify from Child Benefit
- Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland disqualify
- Most apprenticeships in Wales disqualify (except Foundation level ones, which are approved)

Since he's studying A-levels at sixth form college, I'm assuming he's NOT in an apprenticeship - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct, he's not in an apprenticeship. He's just doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is not in an apprenticeship, so he meets that requirement too.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to meet one of several residency requirements.

**Which of the following applies to you?**

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (and meet certain additional conditions)
4. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Which situation best describes you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I normally live in the UK - I live here with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You normally live in the UK, which meets the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex. You mentioned that Alex lives with you at home, which means you meet the responsibility requirement through the "living with the child" path.

Just to confirm: **Alex lives with you in your home, correct?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the responsibility requirement since Alex lives with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who may have responsibility for him

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (the usual situation)
2. In hospital
3. In care (placed by the local authority)
4. Being fostered

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's living normally at home with me - just the usual situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final check:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you receiving any payments from the local authority specifically for Alex's upkeep or housing costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 **Congratulations! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!**

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the requirements to claim Child Benefit for your son Alex.

### **Why You Qualify:**

✅ **Alex qualifies as a child** - He's 17 years old and in approved full-time education (A-levels)  
✅ **Education requirements met** - A-levels are approved non-advanced education, and he's studying full-time  
✅ **Not working full-time** - Alex doesn't work at all  
✅ **Not receiving benefits** - Alex isn't claiming any benefits in his own right  
✅ **Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship** - He's doing A-levels, not an apprenticeship  
✅ **You meet residency requirements** - You normally live in the UK  
✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - He lives with you at home  
✅ **No competing claims** - No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ **Council not contributing** - The local authority isn't paying towards his upkeep

### **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through your tax return (though you can still claim to protect Alex's National Insurance record)
5. **Payments** - You'll receive payments every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**To apply, visit:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 17 years old, which falls within the 16-19 age range for potential eligibility; (2) He is in approved non-advanced education (studying A-levels at sixth form college full-time, meeting the 12+ hours per week requirement); (3) His education is not employer-funded and he is not married; (4) He is not working 24 hours or more per week (he doesn't work at all); (5) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right; (6) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (7) The claimant normally lives in the UK, meeting residency requirements; (8) The claimant lives with Alex, establishing responsibility; (9) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (10) Alex lives normally at home and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex. Alex qualifies as he is aged 16-19 and in approved full-time non-advanced education (A-levels), is not working 24+ hours per week, and is not receiving benefits in his own right. The claimant meets all requirements: UK residency, living with Alex (responsibility), no other claimant exists, and the local council is not contributing to Alex's upkeep.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 17 years old, which falls within the 16-19 age range for potential eligibility; (2) He is in approved non-advanced education (studying A-levels at sixth form college full-time, meeting the 12+ hours per week requirement); (3) His education is not employer-funded and he is not married; (4) He is not working 24 hours or more per week (he doesn't work at all); (5) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right; (6) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (7) The claimant normally lives in the UK, meeting residency requirements; (8) The claimant lives with Alex, establishing responsibility; (9) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (10) Alex lives normally at home and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex. Alex qualifies as he is aged 16-19 and in approved full-time non-advanced education (A-levels), is not working 24+ hours per week, and is not receiving benefits in his own right. The claimant meets all requirements: UK residency, living with Alex (responsibility), no other claimant exists, and the local council is not contributing to Alex's upkeep.
